lessons.title: Hypothesis Testing & The Detection Problem
lessons.content:
**Detection** means deciding *yes or no*: is a signal really there, or is it just noise? Think of a medical test (do you have the disease, yes or no?), a smoke alarm (real fire, or burnt toast?), or a spam filter (junk, or a real email?). In every case you look at noisy evidence and make a yes/no call.

We write the two possibilities as hypotheses. **$H_0$** (the "null" case) means **nothing is there** — only background noise. **$H_1$** means **something is there** — a real signal hiding in the noise. Because the evidence is noisy, you can never be 100% sure; you are making your best guess.

Every yes/no decision lands in one of **four boxes**. If a signal is truly present and you say "yes," that is a **hit** (also called a *detection*). If it is present but you say "no," that is a **miss**. If nothing is there but you cry "yes," that is a **false alarm**. If nothing is there and you correctly say "no," that is a **correct rejection**. Two ways to be right, two ways to be wrong.

We track two key chances. The **probability of detection** $P_d$ is how often you catch a real signal. The **probability of false alarm** $P_{fa}$ is how often you sound the alarm when nothing is there. A good detector wants $P_d$ high and $P_{fa}$ low.

**Recap:** Detection is a noisy yes/no test. $H_0$ = nothing there, $H_1$ = something there, and every call is a hit, miss, false alarm, or correct rejection. This same idea underlies radar, medical tests, and AI classifiers.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is the "detection" problem?
choices: Deciding yes or no whether a signal is present in noisy data
Measuring the exact size of a known signal
Removing all noise from a measurement perfectly
Predicting the future value of a signal
items.explanation: Detection is a yes/no question: is a signal there or not, given noisy evidence? Measuring an exact value is a different job called estimation (covered later). You can never remove all noise, which is exactly why detection is a best-guess decision.

items.prompt: A smoke alarm goes off but there is no fire, only steam from a shower. In detection language, this is a:
choices: Hit (detection)
Miss
False alarm
Correct rejection
items.explanation: Nothing real was there (no fire) but the alarm said "yes," so this is a false alarm. A miss would be a real fire that the alarm ignored. A hit is correctly catching a real fire; a correct rejection is staying quiet when there is no fire.

items.prompt: What does the hypothesis $H_0$ (the "null" case) represent?
choices: A signal is definitely present
Nothing is there except background noise
The measurement is perfectly clean
The detector has failed
items.explanation: $H_0$ is the "nothing here" case: only background noise, no real signal. $H_1$ is the opposite, where a real signal is present. We never assume a measurement is perfectly clean; noise is always part of the picture.

items.prompt: A disease is truly present in a patient, but the medical test reports "negative." This outcome is a:
choices: False alarm
Correct rejection
Hit (detection)
Miss
items.explanation: The signal (disease) was really there but the decision was "no," so a real case slipped through. That is a miss. A false alarm is the opposite error: saying "yes" when nothing is there.

items.prompt: What does the probability of detection $P_d$ measure?
choices: How often you say "yes" when nothing is really there
How often you correctly catch a signal that is truly present
How much noise is in the measurement
The total number of decisions you make
items.explanation: $P_d$ is the chance you say "yes" when a signal really is present, i.e. how often you catch real targets. Saying "yes" when nothing is there is the false-alarm probability $P_{fa}$, a different quantity.

items.prompt: A detector works by comparing the evidence to a cutoff value called the threshold. If you RAISE the threshold (demand stronger evidence before saying "yes"), what happens?
choices: Both false alarms and detections become less likely
Both false alarms and detections become more likely
False alarms drop but detections are unaffected
Detections drop but false alarms are unaffected
items.explanation: Raising the threshold makes you more cautious, so you say "yes" less often overall. That reduces false alarms (good) but also makes you miss weak real signals, lowering $P_d$ (bad). Both move down together; this is the core tradeoff.

items.prompt: Why is there always a tradeoff between catching real signals and avoiding false alarms?
choices: Because better hardware could remove the tradeoff entirely
Because the signal and noise overlap, so one cutoff cannot perfectly separate them
Because false alarms and misses are the same thing
Because $P_d$ and $P_{fa}$ are always equal
items.explanation: Noisy "signal present" and "noise only" cases overlap, so any single cutoff that catches more real signals also lets through more noise as false alarms. The tradeoff comes from this overlap, not from poor hardware. $P_d$ and $P_{fa}$ are different quantities, not equal.

items.prompt: A factory wants its smoke alarms to NEVER miss a real fire, even if that means occasional false alarms. How should they set the threshold?
choices: Set it very high so the alarm rarely triggers
Set it low so even weak signs trigger the alarm
Remove the threshold entirely
Set it to make false alarms and misses equally likely
items.explanation: Lowering the threshold makes the alarm trigger on even weak evidence, raising $P_d$ so real fires are almost never missed. The price is more false alarms, which they have decided to accept. This shows the threshold is a deliberate choice based on which error is worse.

items.prompt: How does the spam filter example map onto $H_0$ and $H_1$?
choices: $H_0$ = the email is spam; $H_1$ = the email is legitimate
$H_0$ = the email is legitimate (no spam signal); $H_1$ = the email is spam
Both $H_0$ and $H_1$ mean the email is spam
Spam filtering is not a detection problem
items.explanation: $H_0$ is the "nothing special here" case, a normal legitimate email. $H_1$ is the thing we are hunting for, spam. Marking a real email as spam is a false alarm; letting spam through is a miss. Spam filtering is a classic detection problem.

items.prompt: If the probability of detection is $P_d = 0.9$, what is the probability of a miss (a real signal that you fail to catch)?
choices: It cannot be determined
items.explanation: When a signal is truly present you either catch it (detection) or fail to (miss), and these two cover all cases, so they add to 1. With $P_d = 0.9$, the miss probability is $1 - 0.9 = 0.1$.

lessons.title: Comparing the Evidence: Likelihood Ratio, Neyman-Pearson & ROC
lessons.content:
Suppose you must decide yes or no. What is the *smartest* rule for turning noisy evidence into a decision? The answer is wonderfully simple: ask which story explains the data better.

For any reading you get, you can ask two questions. "If a signal really were present ($H_1$), how likely is this reading?" and "If it were just noise ($H_0$), how likely is this reading?" The first number is the **likelihood** under $H_1$, the second is the likelihood under $H_0$. Divide them and you get the **likelihood ratio**:

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)}.$$

In words: "how many times better does the signal story explain this reading than the noise story?" If the ratio is big, lean toward "yes"; if small, lean toward "no." The **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** just compares this ratio to a cutoff: say "yes" if $\Lambda(x)$ is above the cutoff.

How high should the cutoff be? The **Neyman-Pearson rule** gives a clean answer: first decide how many false alarms you can tolerate (say, at most 1 in 1000), then pick the cutoff that catches as many real signals as possible *without exceeding that false-alarm budget*. It is provably the best you can do for that budget.

To picture a detector's whole behavior, sweep the cutoff from strict to loose and plot the **ROC curve** (receiver operating characteristic): false-alarm rate $P_{fa}$ going right, detection rate $P_d$ going up. A curve that bulges toward the top-left corner is a great detector; a curve sitting on the diagonal is no better than a coin flip.

**Recap:** Compare how well each story explains the data (the likelihood ratio), set the cutoff from your false-alarm budget (Neyman-Pearson), and read the whole tradeoff off the ROC curve. AI engineers grade classifiers with the exact same ROC curve.

items.prompt: In plain words, what does the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(x)$ tell you?
choices: How much noise is in the reading
How many times better the "signal present" story explains the reading than the "noise only" story
The exact value of the signal
Whether the threshold is set correctly
items.explanation: The likelihood ratio compares two explanations: it is the probability of the reading if a signal is present, divided by the probability if it is just noise. A large ratio means the signal story fits much better, so you should lean toward "yes."

items.prompt: A likelihood ratio test decides "signal present" when:
choices: The likelihood ratio falls below a chosen cutoff
The likelihood ratio rises above a chosen cutoff
The two likelihoods are exactly equal
The noise level is zero
items.explanation: A large likelihood ratio means the signal story explains the data much better than noise alone, so you say "yes" when the ratio is above the cutoff. Below the cutoff, the noise story is competitive enough that you say "no."

items.prompt: The Neyman-Pearson rule chooses the cutoff by:
choices: Picking the largest possible cutoff regardless of performance
Fixing how many false alarms you can tolerate, then catching as many real signals as possible within that budget
Making detections and false alarms equally likely
Ignoring false alarms entirely
items.explanation: Neyman-Pearson sets a false-alarm budget first (e.g. at most 1 in 1000), then maximizes the detection rate $P_d$ without breaking that budget. It does not ignore false alarms; controlling them is the whole point.

items.prompt: On an ROC curve, what do the two axes show?
choices: Threshold on one axis, noise on the other
False-alarm rate $P_{fa}$ across, detection rate $P_d$ up
Signal value across, noise value up
Time across, amplitude up
items.explanation: An ROC curve plots the false-alarm rate $P_{fa}$ on the horizontal axis and the detection rate $P_d$ on the vertical axis. Each point corresponds to one threshold setting; sweeping the threshold traces the whole curve.

items.prompt: An ROC curve that bulges toward the top-left corner indicates a detector that:
choices: Is no better than random guessing
Catches signals well while keeping false alarms low (a good detector)
Always says "yes"
Has failed completely
items.explanation: The top-left corner is the dream: high detection rate with a low false-alarm rate. A curve bulging that way means the detector separates signal from noise well. The diagonal line, by contrast, is coin-flip performance.

items.prompt: An ROC curve that lies right on the diagonal (from bottom-left to top-right) means the detector:
choices: Is perfect
Performs no better than random guessing
Has zero false alarms
Has infinite signal strength
items.explanation: On the diagonal, the detection rate always equals the false-alarm rate, so saying "yes" is just as likely whether or not a signal is present. That carries no useful information, which is exactly random guessing. A perfect detector instead reaches the top-left corner.

items.prompt: Why do we draw a whole ROC curve instead of reporting a single detection rate?
choices: Because $P_d$ never changes
Because moving the threshold changes both $P_d$ and $P_{fa}$, so one number hides the tradeoff
Because the curve is required by law
Because false alarms do not matter
items.explanation: A single threshold gives just one $(P_{fa}, P_d)$ point and hides how the detector behaves at other settings. The ROC curve shows the full tradeoff as you sweep the threshold, letting you pick the operating point you want and compare detectors fairly.

items.prompt: Two detectors are compared. At the same false-alarm rate, Detector A catches 95% of signals and Detector B catches 70%. On the ROC plot, this means:
choices: B's curve sits higher than A's
A's curve sits higher than B's at that false-alarm rate
Both curves are identical
A is on the diagonal
items.explanation: At a fixed false-alarm rate (same horizontal position), a higher detection rate plots higher up. A catches more (95% vs 70%), so A's curve is above B's there. A higher ROC curve means a better detector.

items.prompt: How does this connect to machine learning?
choices: ML never uses ROC curves
A classifier is a detector, and engineers use the same ROC curve to grade it
ROC curves only work for radar, not software
Classifiers cannot adjust their threshold
items.explanation: A classifier deciding spam vs not-spam is doing detection, and ML engineers sweep its decision threshold to plot the very same ROC curve. The detection rate becomes the true-positive rate and the false-alarm rate becomes the false-positive rate.

items.prompt: What does it mean if the likelihood ratio for a reading equals exactly 1?
choices: The reading is impossible
The signal and noise stories explain the reading equally well, giving no preference
A signal is certainly present
The threshold must be zero
items.explanation: A ratio of 1 means $p(x|H_1)$ equals $p(x|H_0)$, so both explanations are equally good and the data gives no nudge either way. Only when the ratio is clearly above (or below) your cutoff does the evidence favor "yes" (or "no").

lessons.title: Finding a Known Signal: The Matched Filter & CFAR
lessons.content:
Sometimes you know exactly what the signal *looks like* if it is there. A radar transmits a specific pulse and listens for that same pulse echoing back; a phone listens for the precise shape of its wake word. The signal is buried in noise, but its shape is known. How do you find it?

The trick is the **matched filter**, and the idea is just **pattern matching by correlation**. Slide a copy of the known signal shape (a "template") along your noisy data, and at each spot ask: "how well does the template line up here?" Where the data matches the template, the answer is large; where it is just noise, the answer stays small. The spot with the biggest match is your best guess for where the signal is. Correlation lets the matching parts add up while random noise tends to cancel itself out.

Why bother proving it is the *best* filter? Because among all simple (linear) ways of combining the data, the matched filter gives the strongest possible signal-to-noise ratio, the cleanest possible peak. That makes a faint echo easiest to spot.

There is a catch in the real world: the noise level is not constant. Near buildings, weather, or interference, the background is louder; in quiet patches it is softer. A single fixed threshold would then cry wolf in the noisy regions and go deaf in the quiet ones. **CFAR** (constant false-alarm rate) fixes this by *looking at the neighborhood*. For each spot being tested, it estimates the local noise level from nearby reference cells and sets the threshold a notch above that. So the threshold floats up where it is noisy and down where it is calm, keeping the false-alarm rate steady everywhere. (It skips a few **guard cells** right next to the test spot so the signal's own energy does not sneak into the noise estimate.)

**Recap:** When the signal's shape is known, correlate the data with a template (the matched filter) to make the signal pop out of noise; use CFAR to float the threshold with the local noise so false alarms stay constant. A neural-network filter is a *learned* template doing the very same correlation.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what does a matched filter do?
choices: It removes the known signal from the data
It slides a copy of the known signal shape along the data and measures how well it matches at each spot
It guesses the signal shape from scratch
It sets a fixed threshold and never changes it
items.explanation: A matched filter correlates the data with a template of the known signal shape, scoring how well they line up at each position. A big score means the pattern is likely there. It finds the signal; it does not remove it.

items.prompt: Why does correlating with the known shape help pull a faint signal out of noise?
choices: Because noise is louder than any signal
Because the matching signal parts add up consistently while random noise tends to cancel
Because it deletes the noise samples
Because it ignores the signal entirely
items.explanation: When the template lines up with a real signal, the matching pieces reinforce each other and add up. Random noise has no consistent pattern, so it largely cancels itself. The result is a strong peak for the signal against a low noise background.

items.prompt: What is special about the matched filter compared to other simple ways of processing the data?
choices: It is the only filter that uses the data at all
Among linear filters, it gives the strongest possible signal-to-noise ratio (the cleanest peak)
It works only when there is no noise
It guarantees zero false alarms
items.explanation: The matched filter is optimal in the sense that no other linear filter produces a higher signal-to-noise ratio at the output, making the signal peak as clean as possible. It does not eliminate noise or guarantee zero false alarms; it just maximizes the contrast.

items.prompt: A matched filter needs which of the following to work?
choices: Knowledge of the shape of the signal it is looking for
A noise-free measurement
The exact location of the signal in advance
No information at all
items.explanation: The matched filter is built from a template, so you must know the signal's shape (like the radar pulse it transmitted). You do not need to know where it is in advance; that is what the correlation finds. And it specifically works in the presence of noise.

items.prompt: Why does a single fixed threshold fail in the real world?
choices: Because the signal shape keeps changing
Because the background noise level varies from place to place, so one threshold is too high here and too low there
Because thresholds are illegal
Because matched filters do not need thresholds
items.explanation: Real backgrounds are louder in some regions (near clutter or interference) and quieter in others. A fixed threshold then produces too many false alarms in noisy regions and misses signals in quiet ones. The threshold needs to adapt to the local noise level.

items.prompt: What does CFAR (constant false-alarm rate) detection do?
choices: Estimates the local noise level from nearby cells and floats the threshold so the false-alarm rate stays steady
Removes all noise before detection
Uses the same threshold everywhere no matter what
Ignores the neighborhood entirely
items.explanation: CFAR looks at neighboring reference cells to estimate how loud the background is right there, then sets the threshold a notch above it. The threshold floats up in noisy areas and down in quiet ones, holding the false-alarm rate roughly constant everywhere.

items.prompt: In CFAR, why are a few "guard cells" right next to the spot being tested skipped when estimating the noise level?
choices: To save computation time
So the signal's own energy does not leak into and inflate the noise estimate
Because those cells are always empty
To make the threshold higher on purpose
items.explanation: A real signal spreads a little into the cells immediately around it. If those were used to estimate the noise level, the estimate would be falsely high and the threshold would rise enough to hide the signal. Guard cells are skipped so the noise estimate stays honest.

items.prompt: A radar moves from open sky into a region full of rain clutter. With CFAR, what happens to the threshold?
choices: It stays fixed and false alarms explode
It automatically rises to match the louder background, keeping the false-alarm rate steady
It drops to zero
It turns off the detector
items.explanation: CFAR senses the louder local background from the reference cells and raises the threshold accordingly, so the false-alarm rate stays roughly constant even as conditions change. A fixed threshold, by contrast, would let false alarms explode in the clutter.

items.prompt: How does a matched filter relate to a filter in a convolutional neural network?
choices: They are completely unrelated
Both slide a template across the input and respond strongly where the pattern matches; the network learns its template from data instead of being given it
Neural networks never use correlation
A matched filter learns its shape from data
items.explanation: A CNN filter correlates a small template across the input and lights up where the pattern matches, exactly like a matched filter. The difference is that the radar template is known in advance, while the network learns its template from training data.

items.prompt: At the matched filter's output, where do you look to decide whether the signal is present?
choices: At the smallest value, comparing it to the threshold
At the peak (largest match) value, comparing it to the threshold
At the average of all values only
Nowhere; the matched filter gives a yes/no by itself
items.explanation: The matched filter produces a peak where the data best matches the template. You compare that peak to the threshold: above it, declare the signal present. The matched filter sharpens the contrast, but you still need a threshold to make the final call.

lessons.title: Estimation: Pinning Down a Value (MLE, MAP, MMSE & Limits)
lessons.content:
Detection asks *whether* something is there. **Estimation** asks *what its value is*. How tall is someone, given several slightly different tape-measure readings? How far away is a target, given a noisy echo? You want to pin down a number from messy data.

The everyday answer is to **average** your readings. If you measure a height as 170, 172, 169, 171 cm, you would report about 170.5 cm. Averaging works because the random errors push up and down roughly equally, so they tend to cancel. And the more readings you take, the tighter your estimate gets.

Three named recipes formalize this. **Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE)** picks the value that makes the data you actually saw *most believable* ("what true height best explains these readings?"). For simple noisy measurements, MLE is exactly the average. **Maximum a posteriori (MAP)** adds a prior hunch before looking at the data ("I already expect adult heights near 170") and blends it with the readings. **Minimum mean-square error (MMSE)** picks the value that minimizes the average squared error; it is the balanced "center of gravity" of all plausible values.

Every estimate has two ways of being off. **Bias** is being consistently off in one direction (a tape measure that always reads 1 cm long). **Variance** is the jitter, how much your answer bounces around from one batch of readings to the next. Total error is a blend of the two, and there is a hard limit on how small the variance can get: the **Cramer-Rao lower bound** says no honest (unbiased) method can beat a certain noise floor. More data, less noise, and sharper measurements all push that floor lower.

**Recap:** Estimation pins down a value from noisy data, usually by something like averaging. MLE picks the most-believable value, MAP adds a prior, MMSE balances squared error, and the Cramer-Rao bound caps how precise any honest estimate can be. Training an AI model is MLE in disguise; adding regularization makes it MAP.

items.prompt: How does estimation differ from detection?
choices: Estimation decides yes/no; detection finds a value
Estimation pins down a value (a number); detection decides yes/no whether a signal is present
They are the same problem
Estimation only works without noise
items.explanation: Detection answers a yes/no question (is it there?), while estimation answers a how-much question (what is its value?). Estimating someone's height from noisy readings is estimation; deciding whether a smoke alarm should fire is detection.

items.prompt: You measure a length four times: 50, 52, 49, 53 cm. What is the natural estimate of the true length, and why?
choices: 53 cm, the largest reading, because bigger is safer
51 cm, the average, because random errors tend to cancel out
49 cm, the smallest reading
It is impossible to estimate from noisy data
items.explanation: The average (51 cm) is the natural estimate because the random measurement errors push above and below the truth roughly equally and tend to cancel. Picking the largest or smallest single reading would be swayed by one noisy value.

items.prompt: What does maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) choose?
choices: The value that makes the data you actually observed most believable
The value that is largest
A value picked at random
The value with the most noise
items.explanation: MLE asks: which true value would make the readings I actually got most probable? For ordinary noisy measurements, that turns out to be the plain average. It uses only the data, with no prior assumption added.

items.prompt: How does MAP (maximum a posteriori) estimation differ from MLE?
choices: MAP ignores the data entirely
MAP blends a prior hunch (expectation before seeing data) with the readings, while MLE uses only the readings
MAP always gives a larger answer
There is no difference
items.explanation: MAP starts with a prior belief about likely values and updates it with the data, while MLE uses the data alone. If the prior is vague or flat, MAP reduces to MLE. The prior gently pulls the estimate toward values you expected beforehand.

items.prompt: A bathroom scale always reads 2 kg heavier than the truth, no matter how many times you weigh yourself. This is an example of:
choices: High variance
Bias (a consistent offset in one direction)
Zero error
A false alarm
items.explanation: A consistent offset in the same direction is bias. Averaging more readings will not fix it because every reading is shifted the same way. Variance, by contrast, is random jitter that averaging does reduce.

items.prompt: What is the difference between bias and variance in an estimate?
choices: Bias is random jitter; variance is a fixed offset
Bias is a consistent offset in one direction; variance is how much the estimate jitters from batch to batch
They mean the same thing
Both only occur when there is no noise
items.explanation: Bias is being systematically off in the same direction every time; variance is the random spread of your answer when you repeat the measurement. A good estimate keeps both small, and total error blends the two.

items.prompt: What does the Cramer-Rao lower bound tell us?
choices: Estimates can always be made perfectly precise with enough effort
There is a hard floor on how small the variance of any honest (unbiased) estimate can be
Bias is always zero
More data always increases the error
items.explanation: The Cramer-Rao bound is a fundamental limit: no unbiased method can have variance below a certain floor set by the data's noise and how informative it is. You cannot beat physics with clever math alone; you need better or more data.

items.prompt: You take many more measurements of the same quantity. What typically happens to the precision of your estimate?
choices: It gets worse
It improves: more data lowers the variance floor, so the estimate is tighter
It stays exactly the same
The bias disappears automatically
items.explanation: More readings give more information, which lowers the Cramer-Rao variance floor and tightens the estimate (random errors average out further). Note this shrinks random jitter but does not remove a fixed bias like a miscalibrated instrument.

items.prompt: How does training an AI classifier connect to estimation?
choices: Training has nothing to do with estimation
Training picks the model's parameters that best explain the data, which is maximum likelihood estimation (MLE)
Training only uses detection, never estimation
Training maximizes the false-alarm rate
items.explanation: Training adjusts the model's parameters to best fit the training data, which is exactly MLE: choosing the values that make the observed data most believable. A classifier is a detector, and training it is estimation of its parameters.

items.prompt: Adding regularization (a penalty that keeps a model's parameters small) during training is most like which estimation idea?
choices: MLE, because it ignores prior beliefs
MAP, because it blends the data with a prior preference for small/typical parameter values
The Cramer-Rao bound
A false alarm
items.explanation: Regularization expresses a prior belief (parameters should stay small/simple) and blends it with the data fit, which is exactly what MAP does. Plain MLE with no penalty corresponds to using no prior at all.
